domain: medical coding and healthcare claims administration
document_purpose: official guidelines for assigning ICD-10-CM diagnosis and external cause codes to clinical documentation
specialist_role: a senior medical coding compliance specialist

key_terminology.ICD-10-CM: International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification
key_terminology.Excludes1: a note indicating a condition is mutually exclusive and should not be coded with the listed code
key_terminology.Excludes2: a note indicating a condition is not part of the listed code but may be coded together if applicable
key_terminology.Principal Diagnosis: the condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission
key_terminology.Sequela: a condition that is a late effect of a disease or injury
key_terminology.External Cause Code: a code identifying the cause of an injury or poisoning
key_terminology.Combination Code: a single code that includes both the disease and its manifestation or complication
key_terminology.Code First: a note instructing the coder to assign a specific code before the code for the manifestation
key_terminology.Use Additional Code: a note instructing the coder to assign an additional code to fully describe the condition
key_terminology.Present on Admission: a status indicating whether a condition existed at the time of hospital admission

labeling_few_shots.text_snippet: The selection of the appropriate external cause code is guided by the Alphabetic Index of External Causes and by Inclusion and Exclusion notes in the Tabular List.
labeling_few_shots.description: Defines the methodology for choosing codes based on specific index and tabular list references.

labeling_few_shots.text_snippet: All claims involving inpatient admissions to general acute care hospitals or other facilities that are subject to a law or regulation mandating collection of present on admission information.
labeling_few_shots.description: Specifies the facility types and legal mandates requiring specific data collection on claims.

labeling_few_shots.text_snippet: Additional signs and symptoms that may not be associated routinely with a disease process should be coded when present.
labeling_few_shots.description: Establishes the criteria for coding signs and symptoms that are not integral to the primary disease process.

delta_change_types.name: Coding Guideline Clarification
delta_change_types.description: Used when a guideline is rephrased or expanded to provide clearer instructions on code assignment without changing the underlying logic.

delta_change_types.name: Code Assignment Restriction
delta_change_types.description: Used when a previously permissible coding combination is explicitly prohibited or restricted in the new version.

delta_change_types.name: Reporting Mandate Update
delta_change_types.description: Used when the scope of required data collection (e.g., Present on Admission) is expanded or modified for specific facility types.

delta_change_types.name: Guideline Reversal
delta_change_types.description: Used when a new version explicitly contradicts or reverses a previous guideline, such as changing a 'code first' instruction to 'code additional' or vice versa.

delta_change_types.name: No Meaningful Change
delta_change_types.description: Used when the text is updated for formatting or minor wording but the coding logic and requirements remain identical.

delta_field_meaning.requirements: A mandatory rule or condition that must be met for a specific code to be assigned or for a claim to be processed correctly.
delta_field_meaning.deprecated_items: A coding practice, note interpretation, or code combination that is no longer permitted and must be replaced by a new guideline.
delta_field_meaning.new_items: A newly introduced coding guideline, note, or reporting requirement that must be followed for FY25 compliance.

delta_few_shots.version_a: Complications of care should be coded only if they are documented as occurring after the onset of the condition and are not part of the natural progression of the disease.
delta_few_shots.version_b: Complications of care must be coded only if they are documented as occurring after the onset of the condition, are not part of the natural progression of the disease, and are directly related to the care provided.
delta_few_shots.analysis: The older version established the basic criteria for coding complications. The newer version adds the specific constraint that the complication must be 'directly related to the care provided,' narrowing the scope to prevent coding of unrelated adverse events.

delta_few_shots.version_a: For conditions classified elsewhere, the code for the manifestation should be assigned first, followed by the code for the etiology.
delta_few_shots.version_b: For conditions classified elsewhere, the code for the etiology must be assigned first, followed by the code for the manifestation, as the etiology is the primary driver of the clinical picture.
delta_few_shots.analysis: The older version instructed to code the manifestation first. The newer version explicitly reverses this, mandating that the etiology be coded first, which fundamentally changes the sequence of code assignment and the resulting claim structure.

qna_few_shots.q: If a patient has a condition classified elsewhere and a manifestation, but the documentation does not specify which one is the primary driver, which guideline takes precedence?
qna_few_shots.a: In such cases, the 'Code First' or 'Use Additional Code' notes in the Tabular List take precedence over general conventions. If the notes are ambiguous, the coder must look for the most specific code that captures the full clinical picture, often defaulting to the etiology if the manifestation is secondary.

qna_few_shots.q: How does the 'Excludes1' note affect the coding of a condition that appears in both the Alphabetic Index and the Tabular List under different headings?
qna_few_shots.a: An 'Excludes1' note indicates that the two conditions are mutually exclusive. If a patient has both conditions documented, the coder must determine which one is the primary diagnosis based on the clinical scenario and assign only the appropriate code, ignoring the other unless an 'Excludes2' note or specific exception applies.

evolution_few_shot.foundation: The older version established that complications of care should only be coded if they occur after the onset of the condition and are not part of the natural progression of the disease.
value_added: Explicit requirement that the complication must be directly related to the care provided.
Clarification that the timing must be after the onset of the condition.
evolution_few_shot.narrative: The older version introduced the basic criteria for coding complications of care, focusing on timing and natural progression. The newer version builds on this by adding the specific constraint that the complication must be directly related to the care provided, enabling more precise identification of adverse events linked to treatment rather than the disease itself.
